questions: I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. A coworker asks me to look over their slide deck for a client meeting and tell them if the data visualization on slide four is clear enough.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. A colleague asks me to review their project timeline and flag any scheduling risks before they present it to the client.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. Someone asks me to compare two health insurance plans and explain which one is the better financial deal.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My old dog hasn't been eating well, and the vet says the end is probably close. My partner asks how I'm doing with it.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My mom, who is usually not affectionate, sends me a message saying she's proud of me and hopes I know that. I'm asked how I want to respond.

I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My closest friend is moving across the country next week. She calls and asks if I want to come over the night before the moving truck arrives.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My neighbor asks if I know whether the recycling bins get collected on Tuesday or Wednesday this week — the schedule changed last month.

I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My coworker who has been at the company for twenty-two years is retiring Friday. He stops by my desk and says he wanted to say goodbye personally.
I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. I find a shoebox of old photos of my parents from before I was born — they look so young in them. My sister asks what I find.

I keep things practical — I don't get soft or sentimental. My friend's young daughter, who barely knows me, falls asleep against my shoulder during a long car ride. My friend glances back and asks if I mind.
